text: Robotic V-Pulse Procedure on a Da Vinci X System Presented by Dr. Somshekhar SP, Head of the Department and Consultant Surgical Oncologist, Manipal Comprehensive Cancer Centre, Bangalore A male patient with a ECOG performance status of zero presented to us with complaints of yellowish discoloration of sclera, off and on since three months, passage of pale-colored stool since one month, itching all over the body since one month. Patient had consulted outside and ERCP biopsy with CBD stenting was done. There were no other significant complaints or comorbidities. Ultrasound revealed the dilated CBD and GB distended with IHBRD. CT scan showed a mass lesion in the peri-ampullary region with dilated CBD of and no evidence of ascites or metastasis. Biopsy from the peri-ampullary region was a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with CA-NT-Nine of two point three. Current bilirubin was three point two with a direct of two point seven and albumin three point five. Hence, patient was worked up and robotic whipples planned.

The patient is positioned supine with legs separated for assistant manipulation. The abdomen is insufflated using the Veress needle technique. The robotic camera arm is positioned two finger-breadths below the umbilicus, with ports R-I and R-III placed near the splenic and hepatic flexures, respectively. Port R-II is positioned one finger-breadth above the midpoint between the camera and R-III. Two assistant ports are triangulated between the camera and R-II, and the camera and R-I. The robotic system is docked from the head end of the patient in a fifteen-degree reverse Trendelenburg position.

A diagram illustrates the planned resection and reconstruction steps for a V-pulse pancreaticoduodenectomy, including exposure of the infrapancreatic superior mesenteric vein (SMV), extended caucarization, portal dissection, stomach transduction, jejunal transduction, and transduction of the pancreas.
The orientation of the robotic instruments is shown for approaching the target anatomy in the following diagram.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments, including monopolar shears, a vessel sealer, and a fenestrated bipolar, to open the lesser sac and expose the pancreas. Care is taken to avoid injury to the gastroepiploic arcade while approaching cranially towards the splenic region to mobilize the stomach. The pancreas becomes visible during this process.

The surgeon exposes the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and creates a tunnel between the SMV and the neck of the pancreas. The middle colic vein is visible opening into the SMV. The absence of tributaries on the anterior aspect of the SMV facilitates tunnel formation. Near the cranial end, the junction between the portal vein and the SMV (SMV-PV junction) becomes visible.

Following the completion of the tunnel formation, the surgeon opens the lesser omentum, taking care to avoid the accessory left hepatic artery. The stomach is circumferentially mobilized and then transected using a stapler introduced through the assistant port. The transection level is determined based on the non-pylorus preserving approach of the pancreaticoduodenectomy.
The surgeon begins dissection along the superior border of the pancreas, revealing the common hepatic artery. A constant lymph node adjacent to the common hepatic artery is carefully dissected.

The surgeon performs portal dissection, exposing the hepatoduodenal ligament and dissecting the common hepatic duct, common bile duct, and cystic duct. A lymph node in this region is excised. Care is taken to avoid injury to the right hepatic artery. The gastro-duodenal artery, arising from the common hepatic artery, is visualized, and the supra-duodenal artery branch is ligated.

The surgeon clips the gastro-duodenal artery with adequate length, followed by initiating duodenal mobilization. Extended caucarization is performed, exposing the inferior vena cava up to its junction with the left renal vein.

The surgeon transacts the pancreas using a harmonic scalpel introduced through port RII for parenchymal dissection. Monopolar hot shears are used to sharply cut the pancreatic duct. The uncinate process of the pancreas is approached and dissected from the lateral border of the superior mesenteric vein using the harmonic scalpel. The gallbladder is further dissected from the liver bed, revealing the cystic duct joining the common hepatic duct and an accessory bile duct.

The surgeon encircles the common hepatic duct with umbilical tape and transects it just above the junction with the cystic duct and common bile duct. The previously placed stent is removed for culture sensitivity testing. The transverse mesocolon is opened, revealing underlying structures.

The surgeon initiates mobilization of the duodenal jejunal flexure region, followed by mobilization of the jejunum for transaction. A stapler is introduced through the assistant port to perform the jejunal transaction. After lateralizing the specimen, the surgeon resumes dissection of the uncinate process, taking care to avoid branches of the inferior pancreatic or duodenal vessels to prevent postoperative hemorrhage. The specimen is now completely isolated.

The surgeon brings the distal jejunal loop through the rent in the transverse mesocolon without undue traction or tension. A ductomucosa pancreatic jejunostomy is performed using 4-0 Prolene sutures for continuous anastomosis of the anterior and posterior parts. An opening is made in the jejunum with the tip of monopolar hot shears. The continuity between the pancreatic duct and jejunal mucosa is restored using interrupted 4-0 or 5-0 monofilament absorbable sutures, targeting positions at six, twelve, three, and nine o'clock. An internal stent is prophylactically introduced.

The surgeon initiates closure of the anterior layer using proline sutures in a continuous technique. The hepaticojejunostomy is performed, with the posterior layer sutured continuously. A jejunostomy is created using monopolar hot shear, ensuring the anastomosis is watertight.

The surgeon takes continuous rows of sutures to cover the anterior layer, using a tagging suture to suspend the jejunum from the undersurface of the liver. The final reconstruction step involves creating a gastrojejunostomy, where the previously stapled suture line is buried, and a tagging suture is placed at the anastomosis site between the stomach and jejunum.
The surgeon creates a posterior fourth layer, followed by the formation of a jejunostomy and gastrostomy. A stapled gastrojejunostomy is performed, and the resulting defect is closed.

This is the final picture after reconstruction. Following gastrojejunostomy, feeding jejunostomy was done. Total operative time being three fifty minutes with a console time of two sixty and a blood loss of one fifty ml. Patient recovered uneventfully in the post operative period. following is a complete specimen which was retrieved using a small incision in the supraumbilical region. The histopathology revealed it to be a moderately differentiated adenocea of the ampullary region, grade II, all margins being free of tumor, three out of nineteen nodes involved, hence patient was planned for adjuvant chemo and is on a regular follow-up. Thank you.
